ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Green Peas Masala; closest parent 'pea dish' (FOODON_03302596) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Green Peas Masala curry; 'Peas, cooked' (A0CKP) used as closest parent. Spiced tomato sauce components are not captured.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for Green Peas Masala as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Green Peas Masala is approximately 200 g, typically served as a side dish with rice or Indian breads such as roti or naan.

nutrient_source.title: Peas, green, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170420); adapted with tomato-based masala sauce adjustment using ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (Matar curry, cooked)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macro and micronutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 entry for 'Peas, green, cooked in curry' (matar curry), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170420 for cooked green peas. The masala sauce (tomato, onion, spices, oil) contributes additional energy, fat, sodium, and micronutrients. Values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; green peas (cooked) GI = 48
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 48 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for cooked green peas. Green Peas Masala is a cooked pea dish; the masala sauce (tomato, onion, spices) does not substantially alter the GI of the pea base. GI is applied as an analogous estimate for the finished dish. GL per serving calculated as (48 × 11.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving / 100) / 100 = approximately 11.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of portion size, as a 200 g serving contributes approximately 640 mg sodium; requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting portion to 100–150 g can help manage daily sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 48) and high dietary fibre (4.1 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Green Peas Masala has a low GI of 48 and provides 4.1 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (200 g), as the fibre and protein content slow glucose absorption and support postprandial glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g), high dietary fibre, and plant-based protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Green Peas Masala is low in saturated fat and cholesterol-free, with a favourable unsaturated fat profile from cooking oil; its high fibre content (4.1 g per 100 g) supports LDL cholesterol reduction, making it a heart-friendly choice when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Peas Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian dish consisting of tender green peas (matar) simmered in a richly spiced tomato-onion gravy. The sauce is built by sautéing onions, garlic, and ginger in oil, then adding tomatoes and a blend of ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, chilli powder, and garam masala. The dish has a vibrant orange-red colour, a mildly spicy and tangy flavour profile, and a thick, hearty texture. It is commonly served as a side dish with roti, naan, paratha, or steamed basmati rice. Nutritionally, Green Peas Masala is a good source of plant-based protein (4.5 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (4.1 g per 100 g), iron, folate, and vitamin C. Its low glycemic index (GI 48) and high fibre content make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking heart-healthy meal options. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal. In Singapore, it is commonly found on the menus of North Indian restaurants and is often paired with bread-based accompaniments. It is also known as Matar Masala or Mutter Masala in Hindi-speaking communities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Green Peas Masala?
Is Green Peas Masala good for diabetics?
What is the GI of Green Peas Masala?
Is Green Peas Masala high in sodium?
What spices are in Green Peas Masala?
What to eat with Green Peas Masala?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Indian Food Composition Tables 2017 (matar curry) and USDA SR Legacy (cooked green peas), which are approved primary sources. No exact entry for 'Green Peas Masala' as a restaurant dish exists in any approved source; values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked green peas and applied as an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from ICMR-NIN 2017 (matar curry) and USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170420 (cooked green peas). No exact restaurant-grade entry for Green Peas Masala exists in approved sources. 2. Sodium content (320 mg per 100 g) is an estimate reflecting typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may be lower. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA sources for plant-based legume dishes. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as green peas and tomato-based masala sauce contain negligible vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as the dish is prepared with vegetable oil and contains no hydrogenated fats; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; naturally zero. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 2.1 estimated from oil composition), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.1 estimated). 8. GI of 48 applied from University of Sydney GI Database for cooked green peas (Atkinson et al. 2008); GL per serving = (48 × 11.5 × 200) / (100 × 100) = 11.04, rounded to 11.0. 9. FoodOn match type is closest_parent; no exact FoodOn term for Green Peas Masala exists. 10. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Peas, cooked) used as closest parent. 11. SNOMED CT: no suitable concept found for this prepared dish. 12. This record was sourced from field survey data (Anjana Kitchen - Six Battery Road); the delivery platform data was used only for food identity and restaurant context, not for nutrient values.
